lens.included_activities: Privately operated U.S. line-haul freight rail services moving cargo over main and branch lines between terminals, with repeat shipper relationships and recurring transportation demand.
lens.excluded_activities: Public intercity passenger operations, local switching and terminal railroads classified in NAICS 482112, tourist or club operations, and software-only logistics coordination.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Freight shippers buy recurring physical transportation capacity through negotiated contracts and tariffs; revenue varies with carloads, lanes, commodity mix, core pricing, service commitments, and fuel-surcharge mechanisms.
lens.deviation_from_default: The default U.S. lower-middle-market recurring outsourced-service lens is narrowed to freight because intercity passenger operations have structurally different public ownership, consumer-fare, and policy economics. The narrowing is for analytical coherence, not attractiveness.

a.rationale: The direct occupational evidence is available only at four-digit NAICS 482100. It shows a labor base dominated by transportation and material-moving work, including rail transportation workers, plus substantial installation, maintenance, repair, construction, and extraction employment. Those physical and safety-critical tasks limit substitution. The automatable share is concentrated in dispatch support, documentation, billing, planning, exception triage, inspection-data review, and maintenance diagnostics rather than train handling, field maintenance, or mandated crew activity.
a.change_evidence: A six-digit 482111 staffing distribution, task-level time-use study, or railroad deployment study showing production labor hours eliminated by AI-enabled dispatch, inspection, or maintenance systems would materially change the estimate.
caveats: The BLS table covers NAICS 482100 and therefore can include employment outside 482111.
Occupational shares are not task shares; even exposed occupations contain physical, regulated, and exception-heavy work.
Automated inspection evidence establishes technical capability, not realized labor-hour displacement.
a.bridge: BLS NAICS 482100 is used as the nearest occupational proxy for 482111 because no six-digit staffing table was located. The proxy is translated through railroad task content: office, analytical, dispatch-support, and inspection-review work is more exposed, while train crews, field maintenance, and track work remain substantially human and physical. FRA crew and inspection rules constrain the upper bound.

rho.rationale: Railroads have strong incentives to adopt analytical automation in network planning, inspection, maintenance prioritization, customer service, and back-office workflows. Realization is constrained by safety regulation, unionized work rules, legacy operating systems, capital cycles, interoperability needs, and the operational cost of errors. FRA's two-person crew rule and its description of automated track inspection as supporting corrective action and maintenance planning indicate augmentation and workflow compression more clearly than wholesale substitution.
rho.change_evidence: Audited railroad disclosures linking AI deployments to sustained labor-hour reductions, or regulatory changes allowing materially broader unattended operations, would move the realization estimate. Evidence of failed integrations, safety incidents, or stricter staffing mandates would lower it.
caveats: The estimate applies to the automatable task share, not total employment or total cost.
Class I adoption experience may overstate the integration capacity of smaller operators.
Regulatory and labor arrangements can vary by operation and may change during the horizon.

e.rationale: No defensible population of independent U.S. NAICS 482111 operators in the approximately $1 million to $10 million EBITDA band was established. The industry is dominated by very large Class I carriers, while short-line operators are separately classified in 482112. Without a size-matched establishment or enterprise denominator, an eligible share would be manufactured rather than measured.
e.change_evidence: A current Census SUSB or Business Register extract at six-digit NAICS cross-walked to enterprise revenue and EBITDA, or an audited STB carrier roster with size-matched financials, would permit a defensible eligible-population estimate.
caveats: STB carrier classes are defined by operating revenue, not EBITDA.
Establishments are not interchangeable with independent acquisition targets.
The frozen dataset provides no usable value for the related population primitive.

s5.rationale: A five-year sale or control-transfer probability cannot be estimated without first identifying the eligible lower-middle-market operator population. Public railroad combinations, STB control proceedings, and transactions involving short-line operators do not provide a representative numerator or denominator for six-digit NAICS 482111 targets in the specified EBITDA band.
s5.change_evidence: A verified five-year panel of private 482111 control transfers matched to a complete, size-qualified operator roster would support an estimate. The panel would need to distinguish asset purchases, leases, and operating agreements from genuine changes of control.
caveats: Rail asset transfers and trackage-rights arrangements are not necessarily business sales.
Observed large-carrier transactions would not represent lower-middle-market turnover.
Regulatory filings may omit small private transactions or encode transaction type inconsistently.

q.rationale: Line-haul rail pricing is negotiated through shipper relationships, contracts, commodity and lane mix, and fuel-surcharge mechanisms. Network scarcity, captive traffic, and industry concentration can allow carriers to retain a meaningful share of productivity gains. Retention is reduced by trucking and intermodal competition, customer incentives, service commitments, contract repricing, regulatory scrutiny, and fuel-related pass-throughs. Union Pacific's disclosures show that core pricing can coexist with customer incentives, mix effects, and lower fuel surcharges, supporting partial rather than complete retention.
q.change_evidence: Contract-level evidence separating productivity-linked margin improvement from fuel, mix, and volume effects would materially tighten the estimate. Broader reciprocal-switching rights or stronger shipper alternatives would lower retention; persistent network scarcity and demonstrated margin capture would raise it.
caveats: Public Class I economics may not transfer cleanly to any smaller eligible operator.
Fuel surcharges and commodity mix can obscure underlying price realization.
Captive-shipper conditions vary materially by origin, destination, commodity, and access to alternative modes.

d5.rationale: Federal freight planning and the current Freight Analysis Framework support a positive long-run freight-demand backdrop, but a five-year operator-level quantity path remains uncertain. Rail demand is exposed to commodity cycles, export coal, industrial production, intermodal competition, reshoring patterns, service quality, and lane mix. The base case therefore assumes modest cumulative growth, with a contraction case for adverse commodity and modal-share outcomes and an upside case for stronger intermodal and industrial volumes.
d5.change_evidence: A current lane- and commodity-level volume forecast matched to 482111 revenue exposure would tighten the range. Sustained carload and intermodal gains would raise it; recession, coal decline, service deterioration, or persistent trucking share gains would lower it.
caveats: National freight forecasts do not map directly to a specific operator's traffic mix.
Tonnage, ton-miles, carloads, and revenue are different measures of service quantity.
Public Class I results can mask divergent commodity and regional trends.

o.rationale: The delivered service requires physical rail infrastructure, locomotives, crews or accountable operators, maintenance, regulatory compliance, and movement over a controlled network. Software can improve planning, inspection, customer interfaces, and asset utilization, but it does not let shippers self-provide line-haul rail movement without a railroad. The principal substitution risk is modal switching or a different rail carrier, not replacement of the operator by software.
o.change_evidence: Evidence of commercially scaled, regulator-approved autonomous rail networks that allow infrastructure owners or shippers to bypass incumbent operators would reduce the estimate. Continued crew, safety, and network-control requirements reinforce it.
caveats: Operator survival is distinct from demand retention; customers can switch carriers or modes.
Automation could alter staffing and asset intensity without eliminating the licensed network operator.
The estimate assumes the service remains physical freight movement rather than a software-only coordination product.

narrative.executive_view: The physical line-haul railroad operator is highly durable and has credible opportunities for AI-assisted workflow compression, but the industry is network- and asset-intensive and dominated by Class I systems. The decisive diligence gap is whether a measurable population of independent NAICS 482111 businesses exists in the requested $1 million to $10 million EBITDA band.
narrative.ai_impact: The most credible five-year path is augmentation and selective labor compression through automated track inspection, machine vision, predictive maintenance, dispatch and network-planning support, document processing, billing, customer communications, and exception triage. Physical train operation, field repair, track maintenance, safety accountability, and regulated crew activity remain much less substitutable.
narrative.value_capture: Network access and captive lanes can protect pricing, while trucking and intermodal alternatives, service reliability, shipper bargaining, contract renewals, incentives, fuel mechanisms, and regulatory remedies constrain capture. AI-derived savings should split among carrier margins, improved service, and shipper pricing rather than accrue entirely to the operator.
narrative.firm_availability: No defensible roster or denominator of independent 482111 operators in the specified EBITDA band was established. Short-line operators are separately classified in 482112, and public carrier or establishment counts cannot be treated as lower-middle-market acquisition targets. Both eligibility and five-year sale incidence therefore remain MISSING.
narrative.demand_durability: A modest positive freight backdrop is plausible, but commodity mix, export exposure, industrial production, service quality, and modal competition make the five-year range wide. Software does not replace the physical rail movement, so operator persistence remains very high even under aggressive workflow automation.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The central risks are absence of a size-qualified target population, concentration in large carriers, commodity and lane volatility, regulatory and labor constraints, legacy-system integration, modal substitution, and reliance on four-digit occupational evidence. Public Class I disclosures may overstate both technology capacity and pricing power for any smaller operator.
narrative.principal_driver: The physical, network-controlled nature of line-haul freight creates very high operator persistence while inspection, planning, maintenance, and administrative workflows offer meaningful but bounded AI-assisted productivity upside.
narrative.principal_weakness: The eligible lower-middle-market operator population and representative five-year transfer history cannot be measured from the available evidence, so firm availability and sale incidence are missing rather than estimable.

sources.title: May 2023 National Industry-Specific Occupational Employment and Wage Estimates: NAICS 482100 - Rail Transportation
sources.supports: Nearest available occupational distribution: transportation and material-moving occupations are 51.50% of employment, rail transportation workers 44.40%, installation/maintenance/repair 15.21%, construction/extraction 11.43%, office/administrative support 4.87%, management 4.30%, and business/financial operations 2.99%.

sources.title: Train Crew Size Safety Requirements
sources.supports: The 2024 final rule generally requires two crew members for covered railroad operations, with identified exceptions and approval or reporting requirements for certain one-person operations.

sources.title: Automated Track Inspection Program (ATIP)
sources.supports: FRA describes automated collection of track-condition information for corrective action and maintenance planning and states that automated inspection technology is used by all Class I railroads.

sources.title: 2022 NAICS Definition: 48211 Rail Transportation
sources.supports: Defines line-haul rail as long-distance movement between terminals and stations on main and branch lines and distinguishes local switching and terminal operations.

sources.title: Economic Data
sources.supports: Documents revenue-based railroad class thresholds, Class I reporting, captive-traffic measures, and Board conditions on fuel-surcharge practices.

sources.title: STB Adopts Final Rule on Reciprocal Switching for Inadequate Service
sources.supports: Describes reciprocal switching as a potential competitive remedy for shippers experiencing inadequate service and notes consolidation and captive-shipper concerns.

sources.title: Union Pacific Corporation 2025 Form 10-K
sources.supports: Shows freight revenue sensitivity to volume, core pricing, commodity and business mix, fuel surcharges, and customer incentives, while reporting productivity improvements at a major Class I carrier.

sources.title: Freight Analysis Framework 8/15/25 Updated
sources.supports: Describes the current FAF release, including annual estimates through preliminary 2024 data and forecasts from 2030 through 2050 across freight tonnage, value, and ton-miles.

sources.title: 2026 National Freight Strategic Plan
sources.supports: Provides the current federal freight-planning outlook and modal freight projections used as directional context for the five-year demand range.
